content:
Now that we have the patchset that drops the alloc-fork, I guess we might want to start basing patches on top of it? Are there expected to be any further versions of it, and would they affect patches based on top of it?

Is there a branch somewhere for making it easy to base patches on top of it?

cc @Wedson Almeida Filho
I guess it would make an appearance in rust-dev at some point?
Well, that has all sorts of other stuff too, so it's less suitable as a base for patchsets.

It's already in rust-dev.

Alice, I will send a new version with a small change to the implementation of one of the functions, but no interface changes. You can find the latest here (including the change): https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/commits/allocator/
(The change in the implementation is a suggestion by Boqun that results in removing an unsafe block.)

Alice Ryhl said:

Well, that has all sorts of other stuff too, so it's less suitable as a base for patchsets.

Hmm... I wonder why? Or put it in another way, what would be helpful to make it more suitable ;-) One purpose of rust-dev is providing a base with "good enough" patches that are not in rust-next yet.

Also, you could pick an commit in rust-dev as your base, for example:

00ecb8e9a898 rust: kernel: remove usage of allocator_api unstable feature
but of course, it depends on your workflow ;-)

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Well, that has all sorts of other stuff too, so it's less suitable as a base for patchsets.

Hmm... I wonder why? Or put it in another way, what would be helpful to make it more suitable ;-) One purpose of rust-dev is providing a base with "good enough" patches that are not in rust-next yet.

Well, if you try to apply the patch on top of v6.9-rc1, then it will not apply if rust-dev happens to touch something that my patchset also touches. So unless something is a true dependency of my patch, I try to avoid basing patches on top of it.

I will sometimes use rust-next for a base, but only for patches that I expect Miguel to take. (And not for things that should go through other trees.)

Alice Ryhl said:

Well, if you try to apply the patch on top of v6.9-rc1, then it will not apply if rust-dev happens to touch something that my patchset also touches. So unless something is a true dependency of my patch, I try to avoid basing patches on top of it.

But sometimes a true dependency may conflict with other patches that your work doesn't depend on, in other words, if these other patches get merged, you work still need to adjust to them.
But in that case the maintainers would have to do the work, not the author of the patches

Benno Lossin said:

But in that case the maintainers would have to do the work, not the author of the patches

I'm not sure about it.
For example, allocator_api changes are not in rust-next yet, does it mean you can always ignore it and let the maintainers do the adjustment for now?

I mean, the reason I base changes on v6.9-rc1 or rust-next is to reduce the work for the maintainer. I opened this thread because this is a case where basing it on v6.9-rc1 or rust-next increases the work for the maintainer, which is a good reason to do something else.

Yeah, I know, so what I meant was if you base on things like rust-dev(i.e. having the dependencies that you may not need), it may help maintainer further, or that's at least what rust-dev tries to do.
